network.name: Skynet Nexus News
network.tagline: Real youth stories, worth reading together.
editorialRules: Youth-first: subjects are usually under 25, quoted first.
Family-safe: no violence, war, politics, celebrity drama, or social media pile-ons.
No mainstream news filler: own reporting, primary sources.
Every article includes Kid Take + Family Discussion + glossary for co-reading.

articles.title: Ziyu Shao, 16, Just Won the 50-Year-Old Bachauer Piano Competition in Salt Lake City
articles.excerpt: The five finalists were all 16 or 17 years old. They played Chopin, Liszt, Rachmaninoff, and Tchaikovsky with a full symphony behind them. And they're calling themselves "the prodigies of tomorrow."
articles.kidTake: In Salt Lake City, Utah, five kids around 16 or 17 years old played the piano in front of a whole big orchestra — the Utah Symphony — in a very famous concert hall. It was the final of a competition that started with 316 kids from 28 different countries. A 16-year-old girl named Ziyu Shao, from China, won first place. All five kids stayed with families in Salt Lake City who let them practice on their pianos every day for two weeks!
articles.author: Riley Chen

articles.title: MIT Students Just Designed a Moon Base — and NASA Handed Them Five Trophies
articles.excerpt: 35 students. Eight departments. One shared blueprint for humanity's first permanent home on the Moon — and NASA judges couldn't stop giving them awards.
articles.kidTake: A bunch of college students at MIT spent a whole school year designing what a real, permanent Moon base could look like — power lines, cell towers, and even little robots that turn Moon dirt into bricks. NASA had a contest for who could design it best, and the MIT students won five awards, including first place. Their ideas might really get used when astronauts start living on the Moon in a few years.
articles.author: Priya Ramanathan

articles.title: Eight U.S. Kids Cracked the Top 10 at the FIDE World Cadet Chess Cup
articles.excerpt: No U.S. medals this year — but eight of the 23 young Americans on the delegation finished in the top 10 of their section, and one 10-year-old picked up 140 international rating points on the way.
articles.kidTake: In a country called Georgia (not the U.S. state — the country next to Turkey!), 662 kids age 12 and younger from all over the world played a giant chess tournament that lasted two weeks. Eight kids from Team USA finished in the top 10 of their age group. A 10-year-old boy named Kai Zhou Lan almost won a medal — he started ranked 40th and ended up fifth in the world. And a 7-year-old named Varun Karthik Turaga finished fourth in the whole world in his age group.
articles.author: Amara Okafor

articles.title: Beaverton Robotics Team Went Viral Chasing Jordan's World Cup Squad. It Worked.
articles.excerpt: About 25 high schoolers from four Beaverton, Oregon schools spent June building not just robots, but a viral internet campaign — all to bring FIRST Robotics to a country on the other side of the world.
articles.kidTake: A group of about 25 high schoolers in Oregon build big competition robots. Their favorite World Cup team, from Jordan, came to practice in Portland — so the students made a new short video every day for three weeks asking the players to come visit their robot shop. The videos went so viral that a huge news station in Jordan showed them on TV. The soccer players haven't come by yet, but the students say the real goal was to get more kids in Jordan excited about building robots.
articles.author: Maya Ortiz

articles.title: Welcome to Skynet Nexus - The Signal Goes Live
articles.excerpt: A new daily news network for families who want to learn together - real youth achievements in science, engineering, robotics, creative play, and music. Free forever. First full edition drops tomorrow.
articles.kidTake: This is a new news website! Every morning it gives you four short stories about cool things kids around the world are doing - in science, robots, creative games, and music.
articles.author: The Newsroom
